title: The Agony in the Garden
subtitle: Compline: Christ enters Gethsemane and is sorrowful unto death

stillness: He withdrew apart to pray, and prayed alone.
Cyril says: he prays apart, that you may learn
that we must speak with the high God
with attentive mind and quiet heart.
Be still, then. Seek much solitude.
gospel: Then Jesus came with them into a place called Gethsemane.
And when he had entered the garden he said to his disciples:
Sit here, and do not be moved,
while I go yonder and pray.
And do you also pray, lest you enter into temptation.
He took with him Peter and the two sons of Zebedee,
and he began to be sorrowful and heavy.
Then he said to them: My soul is sorrowful unto death.
Sustain here, and watch with me.
And being in agony, he prayed the longer.
And his sweat became as drops of blood, running down upon the ground.
behold: Take up again these meditations from the beginning of the Passion,
and follow them in order to the end;
and attend to each thing as if you were present.
Behold, then, the Lord Jesus attentively,
as he goes out from the supper at the hour of Compline
into the garden with his disciples,
now to be accompanied by them on this last journey;
how tenderly, how companionably, how familiarly he speaks to them,
Behold him, whose are all things, in whom the adversary had nothing,
begin, with death now near, to be sorrowful and heavy.
From afar, behold how he has taken our weakness upon himself.
See how he whose are all things began to fear and to be heavy.
consider: Jerome says: in this fearing and sorrowing we are taught,
before the sentence of death, what we who are weak should feel.
This sorrow of the Lord was the greatest there could be,
for his sorrow was as intense as it was possible to be.
Naturally he shuddered at death, and at the most bitter pains,
which he foreknew he would soon suffer.
Yet not as in us were these sufferings in him:
in us such passions run ahead of reason and the command of the will;
in Christ they always followed reason and will.
Willing, he hungered and thirsted; willing, he feared and was sorrowful.
Nothing in Christ is reckoned as forced; all is believed to be voluntary.
The Evangelists say he began to be sorrowful, he began to fear —
not that he was overcome, for the motion did not master him,
but at once he took it up and subjected it to the divine will.
He was sorrowful not only at the death his nature abhors and refuses,
but also for the wretchedness of Judas, and the scandal of the disciples,
the casting-down of the Jews, and the overthrow of Jerusalem —
pitying these, and suffering with them.
Lord Jesus, ruler and master — whence this so violent sorrow of your soul?
Whence this anguish of sweat, and this so anxious supplication?
Did you not offer yourself wholly a willing sacrifice to God the Father,
and bear nothing unwillingly? Truly, Lord.
We judge that you took this also upon yourself
for the consolation of your weak members,
lest any despair if the flesh murmurs where the spirit is ready.
By such signs we are taught that you truly bore our griefs.
prayer: who for me, a wretch, willed to be sorrowful and heavy,
grant me to bear all my sorrows unceasingly toward you;
and may you deign to bear them with me,
O Jesus, who being set in prayer willed to be strengthened by an angel,
grant me, by the power of your prayer,
that your angel may always stand by me as I pray, strengthening me.

latinJewel.latin: Tristis est anima mea usque ad mortem.
latinJewel.english: My soul is sorrowful unto death.
